title: Providencia, Colombia: The Island Time Forgot
dek: A first-hand guide to Colombia's tiny Caribbean island — flights via San Andrés, shark dives, and the Sea of Seven Colours.
author: Pamela Sarti
seoTitle: Providencia, Colombia: The Caribbean's Best-Kept Secret
seoDescription: How to get to Providencia, what to do, when to go, and why this tiny Colombian island on a UNESCO reef still feels like a secret. A first-hand guide.
keyFacts: Where: A Colombian island in the Caribbean, part of the San Andrés & Providencia archipelago.
Getting there: No direct flights from mainland Colombia — fly to San Andrés, then ~20 min on Satena to El Embrujo airport.
The reef: Second-largest barrier reef in the Caribbean, inside the UNESCO Seaflower Biosphere Reserve (2000).
Best time: Drier season mid-January to April (March driest).
body:
I have a thousand videos from this trip. A thousand. And not one of them does justice to how beautiful Providencia actually is. I keep telling people it felt like landing inside an episode of Lost — they laugh, and then I show them the footage of the plane coming in low over the water, and they stop laughing.

How to get to Providencia (the arrival that ruins you for every other arrival)

First, the logistics, because they shape the whole trip: you cannot fly directly to Providencia from mainland Colombia. You first fly into San Andrés — the bigger sister island — on a normal jet from Bogotá, Medellín, or Cartagena. And then, from San Andrés, you board something that looks less like a commercial flight and more like a toy.

The airline you book this leg with is Satena, the state-owned national carrier (some flights are physically operated by a small charter company), flying tiny propeller planes — most seat somewhere from under 10 up to about 19 people. The hop takes about twenty minutes, from San Andrés to Providencia's wonderfully named El Embrujo airport ("The Enchantment," which, honestly, fair).

I will never forget the approach. The plane drops low over the famous seven-coloured sea — turquoise lagoons and coral reefs sliding past the window before you've even touched down — toward a thin strip of runway perched right at the water's edge. For a few seconds it genuinely looks like you'll land in the sea instead of on tarmac. Then the wheels touch, smooth as anything, and the whole little cabin exhales. I've taken a lot of flights in a lot of places, and that one I'll never forget.

Real talk for your trip: book the San Andrés–Providencia flight as early as you can. Only a few daily departures on small aircraft, seats genuinely limited — especially in the drier high season. There's also a catamaran a few times a week (≈3.5 hours over open Caribbean), but I flew, and I'd fly again. One more thing: you pay a tourist card / tourist tax to enter the archipelago — budget for it.

How big is Providencia? Small enough to circle in under an hour

Let that sink in: you can drive a full lap of Providencia in well under an hour — the coastal ring road is only about 17 km, so a relaxed loop in a golf buggy is roughly 40 minutes to an hour. The island itself is around 17 square kilometres, and together with tiny Santa Catalina (linked by a painted footbridge) it holds roughly 5,000 people. No resort chains, no tour buses, no cruise crowds. You rent a buggy, circle the island, and the same faces start to wave at you.

The people are Raizal — islanders descended from English settlers and Africans, and they truly own this land, in every sense. You'll hear three languages braided together: an English-based Creole, English, and Spanish. Picture it: I'm in a Spanish-speaking country, and suddenly everyone around me is speaking something that sounds almost like English — except I can't understand a word of it. It's a different world from Bogotá, salt-aired and reggae-soaked, and that distance is exactly the point.

Diving with nurse sharks on Colombia's biggest reef

We took a boat out to the shark dive site — and let me reassure you right away: it's a spot you go to on purpose, by boat, to scuba dive, not somewhere sharks turn up next to your beach towel. So you can lie on the sand all day without picturing a fin sliding past you. Providencia sits on something world-class for diving: the reef is part of an enormous Caribbean coral system protected within the Old Providence McBean Lagoon National Natural Park, which itself sits inside the wider Seaflower Biosphere Reserve — declared by UNESCO in 2000. This is the second-largest barrier reef in the Caribbean (after Belize), and often ranked among the largest in the world.

And then there were the nurse sharks — the gentle ones, cruising the bottom, completely uninterested in you. We dropped down to them in scuba gear, and they were so sweet. Unbothered, elegant, moving through the water like they owned the place — which, like the islanders, they do.

At some point our guide reached over, took my GoPro right out of my hand, and filmed himself feeding the sharks — totally relaxed, like they were neighbourhood dogs — while I lost my entire mind in the best way. The footage is some of the best I've ever captured, and I didn't even shoot it. Surreal. I felt like I'd wandered onto a film set.

If you go: these dives are run by local operators — and that's a feature. The guides grew up on this reef; they know exactly where the calm nurse sharks are on any given day, the way you'd know which café has the good coffee. Book through your guesthouse or a local dive shop once you're there.

The best things to do in Providencia

  • Cayo Cangrejo (Crab Cay) — a postcard islet inside the national park, ringed by superb snorkeling. The photo that makes friends text "wait, where IS this?"
  • The McBean Lagoon mangroves — you can kayak narrow channels into hidden, glass-still lagoons. (I only found this out afterwards and could have kicked myself — don't make my mistake.) Pure silence, pure green.
  • El Pico — hike the island's highest peak for a view over the whole reef in its impossible blues.
  • Santa Catalina & the bridge — cross the painted Lovers' Bridge to the smaller island and find Morgan's Head, named for the pirate Henry Morgan. Yes, real pirate history.

Aim for the drier months — roughly mid-January to April, with March the driest; the rest of the year (about May to mid-January) is the rainy season, peaking in October. And come knowing the island took a brutal hit a few years back — Hurricane Iota, a Category 5, in November 2020 — and has been steadily rebuilding ever since. On an island of 5,000 people, choosing local guesthouses and local guides is the kind of travel that actually counts.

Why Providencia should be your next trip

Here's what I'd tell you over a coffee, leaning in: there are still plenty of little islands out there the world hasn't gotten to yet — and Providencia is one of the best of them. A genuinely small, genuinely local island sitting on a UNESCO-protected, world-scale reef, that has somehow stayed off the mass-tourism conveyor belt. The journey is part of the magic: you have to want it, connect through San Andrés, squeeze onto a tiny plane, and drop in low over that seven-coloured sea. That little bit of effort is exactly what's kept it the way it is.

You drive the whole island in under an hour. You scuba dive with sharks so gentle a local will feed them right next to you. You hear three languages in a single greeting. You watch the sea turn seven colours. And then you go home with a thousand videos nobody quite believes are real. I felt like I was living inside a story the entire time I was there — go write your own chapter before more people find it.

Read next

  • AlUla, Saudi Arabia: A Desert That Doesn't Feel Real
  • Sixteen Metres Down: Learning to Freedive in Koh Tao

faq.question: How do you get to Providencia, Colombia?
faq.answer: There are no direct flights from mainland Colombia. Fly first to San Andrés (from Bogotá, Medellín or Cartagena), then connect on a ~20-minute Satena flight to Providencia's El Embrujo airport. A catamaran also runs a few days a week, taking about 3.5 hours over open sea.

faq.question: Do you need a tourist card to visit Providencia?
faq.answer: Yes — a tourist card (tarjeta de turismo) is required to enter the San Andrés and Providencia archipelago, paid before boarding. Keep the receipt for departure.

faq.question: When is the best time to visit Providencia?
faq.answer: The drier months run roughly from mid-January to April, with March the driest. The rainy season is about May to mid-January, peaking in October.

faq.question: Can you dive with sharks in Providencia?
faq.answer: Yes. Providencia is known for scuba dives with gentle, harmless nurse sharks at dedicated sites a short boat ride offshore, on the second-largest barrier reef in the Caribbean.

faq.question: How long does it take to drive around Providencia?
faq.answer: The coastal ring road is about 17 km, so a relaxed full loop by golf buggy takes roughly 40 minutes to an hour.

title: AlUla, Saudi Arabia: A Desert That Doesn't Feel Real
dek: Hegra's 2,000-year-old Nabataean tombs, desert horseback rides, and a night under the stars at Desert X — a first-hand guide to AlUla.
author: Pamela Sarti
seoTitle: AlUla, Saudi Arabia: A Desert That Doesn't Feel Real
seoDescription: A first-hand AlUla guide: Hegra's Nabataean tombs, desert horseback rides, Desert X nights, how to get there, visas, and visiting during Ramadan.
keyFacts: Where: Northwest Saudi Arabia, ~330 km by road northwest of Medina.
Getting there: AlUla International Airport (ULH) — direct from Dubai & Doha; domestic from Riyadh, Jeddah, Dammam.
Must-see: Hegra — Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO site, 100+ Nabataean tombs.
Visa: Saudi tourist e-visa via the official Visit Saudi portal; cost & eligibility vary by nationality.
Best time: October–April, ~15–30°C. Summers are extreme.
body:
I genuinely don't know how to explain AlUla properly, so let me warn you up front: I have about a million things to tell you, I'm going to try to fit them into one diary entry, and I'm going to fail. Some places are bigger than the words you have for them.

It's a desert paradise — endless golden sand, and then rising out of it, huge sandstone formations. And then, scattered among all that ancient stone, there are green things growing. Real plantations. I'll get to that. I promise — it's one of the strangest, best parts.

How to get to AlUla — and the e-visa, simply

AlUla sits in northwest Saudi Arabia, about 330 km northwest of Medina by road. There's now an AlUla International Airport (ULH) — direct from Dubai and Doha, and domestic flights from Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dammam. Carriers include Saudia, flynas, flydubai, and Qatar Airways. It's a ~25-minute drive from the airport into town. If you want to combine AlUla with the holy city of Medina, the usual way is to drive — it's about a four-hour road trip, and a popular pairing.

Visa: Saudi Arabia opened up to tourism in 2019 and now offers a tourist e-visa you can apply for online via the official Visit Saudi site, typically valid for one year with stays up to 90 days. Crucially, cost and eligibility vary a lot by nationality and by how you enter — some travellers pay a fee, others (or those entering on certain existing visas) pay little or nothing, and GCC nationals don't need one at all. So don't budget a fixed number — just check the official portal for your own situation before you go. Rent a car too — the sights are spread across the valley.

Hegra: standing before 2,000-year-old Nabataean tombs with nobody around

The crown jewel is Hegra (Mada'in Salih) — Saudi Arabia's first-ever UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the largest preserved city of the Nabataean civilisation south of Petra. Same astonishing carving tradition as Petra in Jordan — but quiet enough to stand alone before a tomb and hear the wind.

You walk up to a sheer rock face and there's a perfect carved doorway, columns, an eagle, weathered inscriptions — two thousand years old, crisp as if the carver stepped away yesterday. Beyond Hegra there's the ancient kingdom of Dadan and Jabal Ikmah, an open-air "library" of inscriptions in languages from a vanished world. I stood there unable to process that humans did this, by hand, twenty centuries ago.

A horseback ride through ancient cities — and plantations in the sand

We went on a horseback ride — AlUla runs beautiful guided rides on gentle, beginner-friendly trails, the kind you can do even if you've barely sat on a horse before. You ride the way the ancient inhabitants once did, past the sandstone formations and the edges of old settlements.

And here's what got me. The whole of AlUla sits in the middle of the desert — and then, mid-ride, we came across the structures of an ancient city with plantations growing right there among them. Real, green, growing crops, in the middle of all that sand and stone. I just sat there on my horse staring at it, because it makes no sense and total sense at once: thousands of years of history, brutal arid land, and people still coaxing food out of it, fed by the same water that made this a stop on the ancient trade routes. That contradiction is AlUla. Nothing here is what you expect.

Desert X AlUla: the night in the desert I'll never get over

We went to Desert X AlUla — an open-air art exhibition spread out across the desert, with land-art installations set right into the canyons and sand. Honestly? At first I didn't quite know what to make of it. But then they brought out a storyteller sharing the region's old tales, and an astronomer to walk us through the constellations — and the whole thing clicked into something I wasn't expecting to feel.

Full dark, no city light for miles, the desert cold settling in, and someone who's studied this sky their whole life pointing up — naming constellations, telling you which stars the caravans steered by, the same stars over the same sand. History and sky, stitched together above your head. I've watched a lot of stars in a lot of countries. This one undid me.

(AlUla's modern landmark, by the way, is Maraya — recognised by Guinness World Records as the world's largest mirror-clad building, a concert hall wrapped in roughly 9,740 m² of mirrors, set into a canyon so it nearly disappears into the desert. Worth seeing for the sheer audacity.)

Visiting AlUla during Ramadan, and as a woman

We visited during Ramadan, and it shaped the trip — for the better. By day, eating, drinking and smoking in public isn't done (it's prohibited by law for everyone), and many restaurants stay closed until after sunset. But the evening transformation is magic: once the fast breaks at iftar, everything turns social and festive and generous.

And the people were so warm. Everyone seemed more open, more tender — and as a woman I was met with genuine, almost protective kindness everywhere. Saudi Arabia is increasingly reported as a safe destination for solo female travellers, and my own experience matched that completely — but honestly, no index told me as much as the daily kindness of the people I met did.

For women travelling here: dress modestly, especially during Ramadan — cover shoulders, knees and midriff; loose, lightweight clothing is ideal for the heat and respectful. The abaya is no longer legally required for non-Muslim women, but it's a nice gesture at times. A headscarf isn't needed for everyday sightseeing, but cover fully (head included) to enter a mosque. Dressing modestly felt less like a rule and more like meeting people halfway — and they met me right back.

Come in the cooler months — roughly October through April (a pleasant 15–30°C). Summer here is brutal; don't fight it.

Why AlUla should be your next trip

Here's what I'd lean in and tell you: AlUla is one of those places the world is only just discovering — and you can feel that you're early. You get Petra-level wonder at Hegra without the Petra-level crowds. You ride a horse past two-thousand-year-old tombs and stare at plantations growing in the sand. You get a desert night so quiet and so full of stars that an astronomer can hand you the whole sky. And the welcome — especially, in my experience, as a woman — is warm enough to make you want to come back before you've even left. Getting there is genuinely easy now: an online e-visa, a short flight, a rental car. I left with a million stories and the certainty I'd only scratched the surface — which is the best feeling a trip can give you.

Read next

  • Providencia, Colombia: The Island Time Forgot
  • Sixteen Metres Down: Learning to Freedive in Koh Tao

faq.question: How do you get to AlUla, Saudi Arabia?
faq.answer: Fly into AlUla International Airport (ULH) — directly from Dubai and Doha, or domestically from Riyadh, Jeddah and Dammam (Saudia, flynas, flydubai, Qatar Airways). It's ~25 minutes by road to town. You can also drive from Medina in about four hours.

faq.question: Is Hegra worth visiting?
faq.answer: Yes — Hegra is Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO World Heritage Site and the largest preserved Nabataean city south of Petra, with 100+ rock-cut tombs from the 1st century BC to 1st century AD, and far fewer crowds than Petra.

faq.question: Do you need a visa to visit Saudi Arabia as a tourist?
faq.answer: Most tourists apply for a Saudi tourist e-visa online via the official Visit Saudi portal, typically valid one year with 90-day stays. Cost and eligibility vary by nationality and entry type, so check the official portal for your case.

faq.question: What's the best time to visit AlUla?
faq.answer: October through April, when daytime temperatures are a pleasant ~15–30°C. Summers are extremely hot.

faq.question: Is AlUla safe for solo female travellers?
faq.answer: It's widely reported as safe and welcoming. Dress modestly (shoulders, knees, midriff covered); an abaya isn't legally required, but full cover including a headscarf is needed to enter a mosque.

title: Sixteen Metres Down: Learning to Freedive in Koh Tao
dek: I almost drowned once. Years later, on a tiny Thai island, I held my breath, dropped into a wall of fish, and learned to stay down longer than I believed.
author: Pamela Sarti
seoTitle: Freediving in Koh Tao: A Beginner's First-Hand Guide
seoDescription: Learning to freedive in Koh Tao, Thailand — courses, costs, how to get there, the safety you must know, and what it's like to face a fear of the water.
keyFacts: Where: Koh Tao, a small island in the Gulf of Thailand — a world-famous dive hub.
Getting there: Boat only (no airport). Bus+ferry from Bangkok via Chumphon (~1h45 crossing), or ferry from Koh Samui.
The course: Beginner freediver, 2–3 days, under AIDA, PADI or SSI.
Cost: ~7,000–8,000 THB, usually incl. manual, equipment & certification.
Depth: Beginners commonly aim for 10–16 m (constant weight).
Safety: Always with an instructor/buddy — never freedive alone.
body:
I should tell you something before this story makes sense: I had a thing about holding my breath underwater. Not casual nervousness — a real, body-deep fear, because I'd once had a near-drowning experience. The kind that rewires you. And then I went to Koh Tao and became the first person in my group to drop into the deep.

How to get to Koh Tao (the island built for diving)

Koh Tao is a small island in the Gulf of Thailand and one of the great diving and freediving capitals of the world. The one thing you must plan around: there is no airport. No buses, no trains, no flights. The only way in is by boat — and honestly, that little bit of friction is part of why the island still feels the way it does.

  • From Bangkok: a combined bus + ferry ticket (Lomprayah, Boonsiri) to Chumphon pier, then a catamaran — about 1h45 to Koh Tao's Mae Haad pier.
  • From Koh Samui (which has an airport): high-speed ferries several times daily. Fly to Samui, then ferry over — the easy option.
  • Also reachable from Koh Phangan, Surat Thani and Chumphon.

Here's something I didn't expect: the island is full of foreigners, and a huge number of them have come alone. Solo travellers from everywhere, all washed up on the same little dive island — which means it has this instant, easy sense of community. You arrive by yourself and within a day you've got a crew: people from your course, people from the next guesthouse over, everyone slightly obsessed with the water. You're never actually alone here unless you want to be.

Freediving courses in Koh Tao: what to expect

You'd think someone with a near-drowning in their past would pick any other activity. But that was the point — I was tired of the fear owning me. Freediving is just you, your body, your breath, and the water. The most direct way to face it.

Koh Tao is one of the best places on earth to do it, saturated with certified schools under AIDA, PADI, and SSI. A beginner course typically runs 2–3 days, usually around 7,000–8,000 Thai baht including manual, equipment and certification. Beginners commonly aim for somewhere in the 10–16 metre range (constant weight) — though it's a goal, not a guarantee: AIDA 2, for instance, certifies from a 12-metre minimum, and how deep you actually get usually comes down to equalizing your ears, not to fitness. And the whole culture is built on safety: you always dive with a buddy/instructor — nobody serious freedives alone — and learning your own limits is the core of the course.

The dive that changed everything

So there I was. First in my group. Heart hammering — not from excitement, from that old fear. I took my breath. I turned head-down. I started to descend. And the water opened — because I sank straight into the middle of an enormous school of fish, a shimmering cloud of them parting and re-forming around my body. So absurdly cinematic I forgot to be afraid.

My instructor descended right alongside me — that's how it works, you're never alone — keeping me calm the whole way. I was so wrapped up in the fish and his steadiness that I lost track of how deep I was going. When I finally registered it, I'd hit sixteen metres. Me — the same person who, not long before, couldn't make peace with holding her breath underwater at all.

Then the fear came back for one sharp moment: I'm out of breath, I need to get up. I flipped so my head pointed up to begin the ascent... and the strangest, most beautiful thing happened. The instant I turned upward, the urge to breathe just — vanished. And here's the thing they teach you, the thing that changed how I understand my own body: that desperate urge to breathe isn't your body running out of oxygen. It's just rising CO₂ setting off an alarm — when you feel it, your blood still holds plenty of oxygen left to use. You don't actually need to breathe yet. It's almost entirely a matter of calming your mind. In that sudden quiet I realised I could have stayed down even longer. The panic had been in my head, not my lungs. The fear had been lying to me the whole time.

I came up slow, broke the surface, and I was a different person than the one who'd gone down ninety seconds earlier.

One crucial safety note, because this matters: "you don't need to breathe yet" is only true early in a calm, supervised dive — it is never permission to push past your limits. The flip side of that physiology is dangerous: hyperventilating before a dive (over-breathing to "feel ready") suppresses the CO₂ that warns you, so the urge to breathe arrives only when your oxygen is already critically low — and a freediving blackout can hit with no warning at all. It's the leading cause of freediving deaths, and it's exactly why this is something you learn properly, in a course, with an instructor. Never hyperventilate, never push your limits, and never, ever freedive alone.

A good freediving course isn't about pushing you deep — it's about teaching you to breathe, relax, read your own signals, and trust a system and a buddy. The depth is almost a byproduct. The real lesson is control. If you have a real fear of the water, like I did — tell your school. The good ones specialise in exactly that.

Planning a freediving trip to Koh Tao

  • Get to the Gulf: fly into Bangkok or Koh Samui.
  • Take the boat: bus+ferry from Bangkok via Chumphon, or a straightforward ferry from Samui.
  • Pick a certified school: AIDA, SSI or PADI; read recent reviews; tell them if you're nervous.
  • Budget 2–3 days for the beginner course (~7,000–8,000 baht) and don't over-schedule.
  • Stay a little longer — Koh Tao is gorgeous above the water too.

Why you should do this

Here's what I'd tell you quietly: Koh Tao is the rare place where you can take one of the most intimidating things imaginable — sinking into the deep on a single breath — and learn it safely, affordably, and beautifully, from world-class instructors, in warm, fish-filled water, over a long weekend. You don't need to be an athlete. You don't need to be fearless. I was the opposite of fearless — I'd had a near-drowning that genuinely scared me off the water. And I still dropped sixteen metres into a wall of silver fish and came up changed. If there's a fear you've been carrying around in your chest, the water off Koh Tao is a very good place to set it down. Go take the breath.

Read next

  • Providencia, Colombia: The Island Time Forgot
  • AlUla, Saudi Arabia: A Desert That Doesn't Feel Real

faq.question: How do you get to Koh Tao?
faq.answer: Koh Tao has no airport — you arrive by boat. From Bangkok, a combined bus+ferry ticket (Lomprayah, Boonsiri) goes via Chumphon pier, then a catamaran ~1h45 to Mae Haad pier. From Koh Samui (which has an airport), high-speed ferries run several times daily.

faq.question: How much does a beginner freediving course cost in Koh Tao?
faq.answer: Around 7,000–8,000 Thai baht for a 2–3 day beginner certification, usually including manual, equipment and certification, under AIDA, PADI or SSI.

faq.question: How deep do beginner freedivers go?
faq.answer: Commonly 10–16 metres (constant weight). It's a goal, not a guarantee — AIDA 2 certifies from a 12 m minimum, and depth usually comes down to equalizing your ears.

faq.question: What causes the urge to breathe — and is freediving dangerous?
faq.answer: The urge to breathe comes from rising CO2, not lack of oxygen, so early on your blood still holds plenty of oxygen. But this is never a reason to push limits: hyperventilating masks that warning and can cause a blackout — the leading cause of freediving deaths. Always learn in a course, never hyperventilate, never freedive alone.

faq.question: Can a nervous beginner with a fear of water learn to freedive?
faq.answer: Yes. Good courses focus on breathing and relaxation over depth, always with an instructor and buddy. Tell your school if you're nervous — many specialise in exactly that.
